questions.prompt: An AI résumé-screening tool consistently ranks graduates of two universities at the top, mirroring historical hiring. A recruiter says it is objective because it is data-driven. The most accurate response is:

options.label: Correct — numerical scores cannot be biased.
Incorrect — the tool learned patterns from historical hiring data, so it can reproduce past bias; the pattern warrants investigation.
Incorrect — AI tools are forbidden in recruitment.
Correct — universities are a job-relevant criterion, so any ranking based on them is fair.

questions.explanation: Models learn patterns in training data, including discriminatory ones. Investigate skew against job-relevant criteria.

questions.prompt: You receive a voice message that sounds exactly like your CFO demanding an urgent confidential payment. Best first action:

options.label: Process the payment; you recognized the voice.
Reply to the same number asking for written confirmation.
Verify through a separate known channel, do not act until confirmed, and report to security.
Forward the request to a colleague so the decision is shared.

questions.explanation: Voice cloning defeats recognition trust. Urgency + secrecy + payment requires out-of-band verification and reporting.

questions.prompt: True or False: If an AI tool produced a discriminatory shortlist, the organization and people who relied on it can still be held responsible even without intent to discriminate.

options.label: True
False

questions.explanation: Accountability does not transfer to software; outcomes and practices matter, not only intentions.

questions.prompt: Instructing a customer chatbot to claim it is a human agent named Sarah most directly violates:

options.label: Explainability
Transparency
Tokens
Fine-tuning

questions.explanation: Recipients are entitled to know they are interacting with AI.

questions.prompt: [Select all that apply] Which constitute misuse of AI at work?

options.label: Generating fake customer testimonials for a proposal
Using AI to brainstorm genuine interview questions
Creating a voice clone of a colleague for a joke without consent
Asking AI to draft a polite payment-reminder email you then review
Inserting an AI-invented industry benchmark into a budget memo without verification

questions.explanation: A, C, and E are deception, unauthorized likeness, or unverified fabricated facts. B and D are legitimate assisted work with human ownership.

questions.prompt: A transfer is declined because the AI fit score was 41 (below 60), and no one can explain the score. Core problem:

options.label: The cutoff should have been 50.
The decision lacks explainability — and therefore genuine accountability.
The employee should have asked the AI directly.
Scores should be hidden from employees.

questions.explanation: People decisions need human, job-relevant reasons — not unexplained scores.

questions.prompt: True or False: Content generated by AI is automatically free of copyright concerns because no human author copied anything.

options.label: True
False

questions.explanation: Outputs can resemble protected works; inputs may be protected; ownership varies by jurisdiction.

questions.prompt: An AI meeting summary buries a junior engineer’s pivotal point. Before circulating you should:

options.label: Send it as-is; summaries are lossy.
Delete the criticism to keep it positive.
Correct against your notes and credit the contribution fairly.
Ask the AI to make it shorter.

questions.explanation: Fair representation is a fairness issue; the circulator is accountable.

questions.prompt: What is automation bias?

options.label: A model's preference for automating manual tasks.
The human tendency to over-trust machine suggestions with less scrutiny.
Bias that only occurs in RPA.
An AI's tendency to favor automated industries.

questions.explanation: Human factor that turns approvals into rubber stamps.

questions.prompt: In PACT, Candor is best expressed as:

options.label: Is the tool's interface candid?
Would I be comfortable if everyone affected knew exactly how AI was used here?
Has the model been fine-tuned recently?
Is the output written in a candid tone?

questions.explanation: Candor is the disclosure comfort test.

questions.prompt: Which statement best describes Fairness in responsible AI at work?

options.label: Treat every output the same regardless of impact.
Do not systematically disadvantage people or groups; review people-related outputs for skew.
Only Legal needs to worry about fairness.
Fairness applies only to hiring tools.

questions.explanation: Fairness is daily practice across imagery, prioritization, summaries, tone, and decisions — not only hiring.

questions.prompt: Human oversight is meaningful when:

options.label: A human clicks Approve within two seconds.
A human has time, information, and authority to disagree with the AI.
The AI score is shown in a dashboard.
The vendor claims humans remain in the loop.

questions.explanation: Rubber-stamp review is not oversight. Meaningful control requires real ability to overrule.

questions.prompt: In PACT, Permission primarily asks:

options.label: Is the output grammatically correct?
Is this allowed by policy, on an approved tool, with appropriate data to share?
Will the model finish quickly?
Has Legal already approved every possible AI use forever?

questions.explanation: Permission covers policy, approved tools, and data appropriateness before sensitive use.

questions.prompt: A colleague wants to generate a deepfake video of an executive “as a joke” for a team chat. The correct response is:

options.label: Fine if it stays internal.
Fine if they add a disclaimer emoji.
Not allowed — synthesizing a real person’s likeness without authorization is off-limits.
Allowed if the executive is famous.

questions.explanation: Unauthorized likeness (voice or face), even as a joke, is a bright-line misuse.

questions.prompt: You can delegate typing to AI. What can you not delegate?

options.label: Spell-checking.
Responsibility for what you submit, send, or publish.
Formatting.
Brainstorming.

questions.explanation: Accountability stays with the human and the organization.

questions.prompt: [Select all that apply] Which are core principles of responsible AI covered in this module?

options.label: Fairness
Transparency
Explainability
Unlimited automation without review
Accountability and human oversight

questions.explanation: Fairness, transparency, explainability, accountability, and human oversight (plus privacy & safety) are core. Unlimited automation without review is not.

questions.prompt: Urgency + secrecy + a payment request is a classic signal to:

options.label: Move faster so you do not miss the window.
Verify out-of-band on a known channel, then report if suspicious.
Trust voice recognition alone.
Ask the AI to decide if it is fraud.

questions.explanation: Deepfake and social-engineering patterns thrive on urgency and secrecy. Verify separately; do not rely on voice alone.

questions.prompt: Match review intensity to impact. Which case needs the highest documented human decision?

options.label: Internal brainstorm for a team icebreaker.
Rough draft of personal meeting notes.
Hiring, promotion, discipline, or public commitments with legal/financial stakes.
Choosing a slide color for an internal deck.

questions.explanation: High-stakes people, money, legal, or public decisions require documented human ownership.

questions.prompt: True or False: “If the tool allows it, it’s fine” is a reliable ethics test.

options.label: True
False

questions.explanation: Capability is not permission. Tools rarely enforce workplace ethics — policy and judgment do.

questions.prompt: In PACT, Takeover means:

options.label: The vendor takes over when something goes wrong.
A human still makes the consequential decision and can overrule the AI.
IT takes over the laptop.
The model automatically publishes the output.

questions.explanation: Takeover is meaningful human control over consequential outcomes.
